westside-proctor.name: Proctor Creek Greenway bioswale corridor
westside-proctor.reason: This is closest to the Westside runoff source and can intercept street runoff before it reaches Proctor Creek.

westside-proctor.name: Maddox Park green soak-in area
westside-proctor.reason: Maddox Park is a real public park beside the Proctor Creek corridor, used here as the visible garden/soil soak-in destination.

westside-proctor.name: Proctor Creek Greenway floodplain storage
westside-proctor.reason: This endpoint sits on a mapped Proctor Creek stream reach, so park detention is shown along the real creek corridor.

westside-proctor.name: Proctor Creek wetland buffer
westside-proctor.reason: Wetland buffers can accept overflow while filtering runoff before it reaches the creek.

westside-proctor.name: Chattahoochee River corridor at Bolton
westside-proctor.reason: The marker is placed on the visible Chattahoochee River corridor, not on the nearby highway crossing.

westside-proctor.name: Maddox Park tree trench recharge
westside-proctor.reason: Tree trenches near Maddox Park can use smaller captured flows for shade and heat reduction.

westside-proctor.name: Mercedes-Benz Stadium stormwater storage
westside-proctor.reason: Stored excess is routed to the real stadium reuse/storage anchor instead of a modeled neighborhood tank.

westside-proctor.name: Northside Drive controlled storm drain release
westside-proctor.reason: Overflow is treated as a last-resort slow release after green storage is full.

south-downtown.name: South Downtown permeable plaza retrofit
south-downtown.reason: Dense pavement produces runoff quickly, so permeable plaza surfaces are the first capture point.

south-downtown.name: South Downtown curbside bioswales
south-downtown.reason: Bioswales along downtown curb lines intercept street runoff before it backs up at drains.

south-downtown.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park soak-in landscape
south-downtown.reason: This is a real green destination with stormwater landscape capacity, replacing the vague Gulch edge marker.

south-downtown.name: Mercedes-Benz Stadium stormwater storage
south-downtown.reason: Downtown excess is routed to the real stadium storage/reuse anchor rather than a modeled Underground Atlanta tank.

south-downtown.name: Urban Food Forest at Browns Mill
south-downtown.reason: Overflow is stored first, then reused for irrigation at a real Atlanta food forest rather than sent directly to crops.

south-downtown.name: Phoenix II Park green storage
south-downtown.reason: Phoenix II Park is a real park south of downtown; the marker is placed on the park, not a road label.

south-downtown.name: South River stream reach
south-downtown.reason: This endpoint uses an OpenStreetMap South River waterway reach, not a road named South River.

south-downtown.name: South Downtown street tree stormwater planters
south-downtown.reason: Tree planters can reuse captured downtown runoff for shade on heat-stressed sidewalks.

south-downtown.name: Clear Creek wetland polishing area
south-downtown.reason: Wetlands provide overflow polishing when downtown storage is full.

south-downtown.name: Downtown controlled drain release
south-downtown.reason: Used only for remaining water after distributed capture and storage are saturated.

old-fourth-ward.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park detention pond
old-fourth-ward.reason: This existing park pond is designed to hold stormwater and release it slowly.

old-fourth-ward.name: Eastside BeltLine rain gardens
old-fourth-ward.reason: Trail-side rain gardens can absorb runoff from nearby paved paths and streets.

old-fourth-ward.name: Old Fourth Ward street tree trenches
old-fourth-ward.reason: Tree trenches turn captured runoff into cooling and canopy support.

old-fourth-ward.name: Old Fourth Ward curbside bioswales
old-fourth-ward.reason: Bioswales can intercept street runoff before it reaches the park pond and downstream drains.

old-fourth-ward.name: Clear Creek wetland buffer
old-fourth-ward.reason: A wetland buffer can polish overflow before it reaches Clear Creek.

old-fourth-ward.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park lake / Clear Creek basin
old-fourth-ward.reason: The recharge marker is placed on the visible park lake that functions as a Clear Creek stormwater basin.

old-fourth-ward.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park stormwater pond reserve
old-fourth-ward.reason: Stored excess stays at the real stormwater pond instead of a modeled community tank.

old-fourth-ward.name: Fourth Ward controlled overflow
old-fourth-ward.reason: Last-resort overflow after the park pond and green infrastructure are full.

vine-city.name: Rodney Cook Sr. Park rain gardens
vine-city.reason: Cook Park is close to the vulnerable residential source and can receive neighborhood runoff.

vine-city.name: Vine City curbside bioswales
vine-city.reason: Curbside bioswales intercept street runoff before it reaches homes and low points.

vine-city.name: Cook Park tree trench network
vine-city.reason: Tree trenches provide both runoff storage and heat reduction in a canopy-gap area.

vine-city.name: Chattahoochee River corridor at Bolton
vine-city.reason: Vine City drains through Proctor Creek toward the Chattahoochee, and the marker now lands on the visible river corridor.

vine-city.name: Rodney Cook Sr. Park detention storage
vine-city.reason: Cook Park storage can slow storm peaks from vulnerable residential blocks.

vine-city.name: Vine City wetland pocket
vine-city.reason: Wetland pockets can receive overflow after rain gardens and bioswales fill.

vine-city.name: Mercedes-Benz Stadium stormwater storage
vine-city.reason: Stored excess is routed to the real stadium storage/reuse anchor near Vine City.

vine-city.name: Vine City controlled drain release
vine-city.reason: Overflow remains only after local green capture and storage fill.

midtown.name: Piedmont Park tree trench recharge
midtown.reason: Midtown runoff can support nearby park trees and reduce urban heat.

midtown.name: Midtown parking-lane permeable pavement
midtown.reason: High impervious cover makes source-control pavement the fastest way to reduce runoff.

midtown.name: Atlanta Botanical Garden rainwater storage
midtown.reason: The Botanical Garden has documented rainwater storage, so the stored-excess marker uses that real destination.

midtown.name: Lake Clara Meer / Clear Creek recharge
midtown.reason: The endpoint is placed on Lake Clara Meer so the waterway recharge point is visibly connected to water on the map.

midtown.name: Lake Clara Meer park storage
midtown.reason: The retention endpoint is placed on Lake Clara Meer, a visible water body in Piedmont Park.

midtown.name: Piedmont Park wetland edge
midtown.reason: Wetland edges can hold overflow and support habitat near the park lake system.

midtown.name: Atlanta Botanical Garden soak-in gardens
midtown.reason: Garden soak-in is anchored to the real Atlanta Botanical Garden beside Piedmont Park.

midtown.name: Midtown controlled drain release
midtown.reason: Used only when distributed storage is full during intense storms.

east-atlanta.name: Brownwood Park stored-excess area
east-atlanta.reason: Stored excess is anchored to Brownwood Park rather than a modeled East Atlanta tank.

east-atlanta.name: Urban Food Forest at Browns Mill
east-atlanta.reason: Stored water can support a real southeast Atlanta food forest during dry periods.

east-atlanta.name: Brownwood Park rain gardens
east-atlanta.reason: Park soils can receive smaller residential flows and keep them out of streets.

east-atlanta.name: East Atlanta tree trench corridors
east-atlanta.reason: Tree trenches use captured water for canopy and neighborhood cooling.

east-atlanta.name: Sugar Creek stream reach
east-atlanta.reason: This endpoint uses an OpenStreetMap Sugar Creek waterway reach instead of a generic corridor point.

east-atlanta.name: East Atlanta wetland pocket
east-atlanta.reason: Wetland pockets can polish overflow from creek-fed drainage areas.

east-atlanta.name: Brownwood Park slow-release storage
east-atlanta.reason: Park storage can pause runoff from rolling residential streets before it reaches creeks.

east-atlanta.name: East Atlanta controlled drain release
east-atlanta.reason: Last-resort release after local storage and park capacity fill.

airport-south.name: South River headwater wetland buffers
airport-south.reason: Large paved areas near headwaters need overflow buffers that can hold and filter runoff.

airport-south.name: Airport-area visible stormwater basin
airport-south.reason: The marker is placed on an OpenStreetMap water basin near the airport instead of an unnamed industrial parcel.

airport-south.name: Flint River headwater stream
airport-south.reason: The endpoint uses a mapped Flint River waterway reach near Hartsfield-Jackson.

airport-south.name: Airport-area permeable service lots
airport-south.reason: Permeable service lots reduce first-flush runoff from large paved surfaces.

airport-south.name: Airport South service-road bioswales
airport-south.reason: Long service-road swales can intercept sheet flow from large paved parcels.

airport-south.name: Airport-area green basin edge
airport-south.reason: Garden soak-in is anchored to a visible basin/green edge near airport facilities.

airport-south.name: South River headwater tree buffer
airport-south.reason: Tree buffers use captured runoff to cool and stabilize headwater corridors.

airport-south.name: Hartsfield-Jackson terminal-area storage
airport-south.reason: Stored excess is anchored to the real airport campus rather than an invented tank location.

airport-south.name: Urban Food Forest at Browns Mill
airport-south.reason: After capture and storage, a controlled reuse share can support the nearby public food forest instead of remaining unused.

airport-south.name: Airport South controlled drain release
airport-south.reason: Overflow is held as the last release path after wetlands and retention cells fill.
